text: The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection in the pelvis, targeting the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The vascular adventitia is cleaned off to isolate the vessel, with the bifurcation of the left colic artery visible near the surgeon's left hand. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is identified distal to the left colic artery, and the surgeon adjusts the angle for optimal access before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection of the left colon, progressing towards the pelvis where dense fibrotic adhesions are visible on the left pelvic sidewall. The ureter is identified posterior to the sponge, and lateral dissection is initiated to connect with the medially dissected space. The surgeon checks the Firefly imaging for ureteral safety and confirms the correct plane before attempting to mobilize the colon from the pelvis.

The surgeon uses scissors for dissection along the left pelvic sidewall, with suction in the left hand to clear smoke, blood, and debris. The Firefly imaging system is frequently checked to ensure the ureter is not inadvertently drawn into the dissection area. The surgeon mobilizes sections of the colon and rectum to facilitate retraction and improve access to other areas.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform a dissection around the loop of sigmoid colon that is adhered to the rectum in the pelvis. The dissection is carefully conducted along the serosa to avoid damaging surrounding structures, including the vagina. The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, revealing the true rectum beneath the fibrotic adhesions.

The surgeon marks the cul-de-sac and plans the distal transection, moving to the right-hand side to continue marking the transection site. On the left-hand side, the surgeon works to reposition the loop of sigmoid colon into the TME plane, navigating through dense, fibrotic pericolic fat and epiploic appendages. The surgeon progresses into the softer tissues of the rectal mesentery, using a vessel sealer to clear the mesentery for transection, preparing to staple distally without transrectal extraction.

The surgeon meticulously cleans the mesentery off the rectal wall to prevent it from getting caught in the stapler or causing bleeding. A 45mm stapler is used deep in the pelvis to staple the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery is then taken while sparing the left colic artery to ensure perfusion. The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery up to the planned proximal margin.

The surgeon checks perfusion with a stapler in place and proceeds to transect the colon, keeping the specimen isolated due to the absence of a prior colonoscopy. A hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis is performed, starting with the back row, followed by a proctotomy and collotomy.

The surgeon begins the hand-sewn anastomosis by placing the inner suture in the center and sewing to the left, ensuring no tension is present. The anterior lip is checked for gaping, indicating tension. The inner anterior row sutures are tied together for security, using seromuscular bites to bring the serosa together. A leak test is performed with a 19mm proctoscope, showing successful distension through the anastomosis. The irrigation fluid is cleared, concluding the procedure.
